FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD study baseline to 2-year follow-up (4.0 release), 8,085 US participants ages 9-10 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Cyberbullying assessed as single-item measure; no separately-measured SM variable with independent coefficient. Applies B3: cyberbullying alone does not qualify.

FT_EC7_reason: Does not meet FT-IC2; cyberbullying alone is not a qualifying SM/smartphone variable
notes: Paper focuses on ACEs and bullying victimization predicting mental health outcomes. Cyberbullying is measured as single item within broader bullying construct; does not qualify as standalone SM variable. No social media time, platform use, or SM accounts measured separately.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study data release 5.0, 8,324 US children ages 9-10 years at baseline, followed annually for 4 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Social media use (time spent on Facebook, Instagram, etc.) measured via Youth Screen Time Survey; qualifies as platform-specific SM use. Separate coefficients estimated for social media vs. gaming vs. TV/videos. Applies B1.

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear mixed models with two-way interaction (DM use × time) testing longitudinal association with ADHD symptoms; three-way interaction (DM use × time × PGS-ADHD) testing moderation. Table 2 reports standardized β coefficients and P-values.

FT_IC4.evidence: Standardized β coefficients (SE) reported in Table 2 for social media × time interaction (β [SE], 0.03 [0.01]; P < .001) and cumulative effect (β [SE], 0.15 [0.03]; P < .001). Additional coefficients in text and supplementary tables.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD longitudinal cohort examining associations between digital media use and ADHD-related symptoms over 4 years

FT_IC6.evidence: Original research article published 2026 in Pediatrics Open Science; no evidence of preprint/abstract/deposit duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Does not apply. Social media is the independent variable (IV) predicting ADHD inattention symptoms (non-SM outcome), not the dependent variable. Paper includes SM → non-SM outcome analysis (B18 applies: SM-as-IV, not SM-as-DV). FT-EC7 requires SM exclusively as DV.
notes: Study examined longitudinal associations between three DM categories (social media, gaming, TV/videos) and ADHD symptoms. Social media use specifically associated with increased inattention symptoms over time (β = 0.03/year, cumulative 0.15 over 4 years). Video games and TV/videos not associated. Cross-lagged models confirmed unidirectional association (SM use → inattention, not reverse). Effect remained after controlling for genetic predisposition (PGS-ADHD) and SES.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD study (referenced in abstract and methods)

FT_IC2.evidence: No PDF available. Abstract mentions 'screen time' as predictor of depression symptoms but does not specify whether it includes qualifying SM variables or only TV/gaming. Cannot determine if separate SM coefficient exists.

FT_IC3.evidence: Not evaluable without PDF; presumed regression analysis based on abstract

FT_EC7_reason: Cannot determine without full PDF access
notes: Paper inaccessible (no PDF). Abstract indicates multi-level predictors of depression in ABCD study with screen time mentioned. Would require full paper to evaluate FT-IC2 (whether screen time includes qualifying SM variables with separate coefficients) and other criteria. Likely excludes if screen time is unspecified/aggregated without per-platform breakdown (B7, B2).
